headings.text: Sea Surface Temperature is Climbing Faster Than Ever Recorded
Heat is accumulating where the water is
SST anomaly by decade · global mean

paragraphs.text: Warming is not uniform. The Arctic Ocean has warmed nearly four times faster than the global mean, while the Southern Ocean's deep mixing has temporarily buffered surface heat. The Tropical Pacific oscillates with ENSO but trends upward decade over decade.

The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation has weakened ~15% since the mid-20th century. Sustained surface warming combined with Greenland meltwater could push the AMOC past a collapse threshold within this century, with cascading consequences for European climate, monsoon stability, and tropical fisheries.
Compare temperature with biodiversity loss, plastic accumulation, and direct action pathways.
An open infographic atlas of peer-reviewed ocean health data for researchers, educators, and policymakers.

items.text:
SST anomaly · 2023
          +1.45°C
          Hottest year ever recorded across the global ocean surface, exceeding the prior 2016 record by 0.15°C and outpacing IPCC mid-century projections.

Ocean Heat Content
          +15ZJ / yr
          The ocean absorbs roughly 90% of excess Earth-system heat. Annual uptake equates to 25 Hiroshima bombs every second, sustained continuously.

Decadal warming rate
          0.18°C / dec
          Surface warming has more than doubled since the 1980s. The post-2000 trend now exceeds 0.21°C per decade in the upper 100m mixed layer.

Marine heatwave days
          +54% / yr
          Frequency of extreme marine heatwaves has risen 54% since 1925. Coral reefs and kelp forests are bleaching at unprecedented intervals.
          SRC
